coredata.dc:title: Disruption of intracellular calcium homeostasis drives graphene quantum dots-induced inflammatory response in liver macrophages

coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), with photoluminescent properties, high stability, and excellent biocompatibility, hold tremendous potential in biomedicine. It is urgent to evaluate their safety and potential health risks to promote their clinical application. Kupffer cells (KCs), as primary immune cells encountered by foreign substances entering the liver and integral to liver immunity, have yet to be systematically studied for toxicity responses to different GQDs. This study focused on three widely used GQDs (OH-GQDs, N-GQDs, and NH2-GQDs), examining their effects on KCs and elucidating the underlying mechanisms. Our findings suggested that the toxicity levels of the three GQDs on KCs are ranked as OH-GQDs > N-GQDs > NH2-GQDs, with inflammation being the main form of toxic effect, which was a consequence of GQD-induced calcium homeostasis disruption. Specifically, cytoplasmic calcium imbalance caused by GQDs leaded to mitochondrial Ca2 + overload, mitochondrial dysfunction, and mtROS generation, which subsequently activated the NLRP3 inflammasome-dependent inflammation. Crucially, we identified upstream mechanistic differences in calcium homeostasis disruption induced by each GQDs, with the most toxic OH-GQDs inducing ER stress-mediated Ca2+ release, which was closely related to the depletion of GSH caused by the generation of oxygen free radicals (•OH and O2•−). By tracing Ca2+ homeostasis, this work comprehensively mapped the upstream and downstream mechanisms of GQD-induced liver macrophage inflammation, providing new insights into the toxic effects of GQDs. Additionally, linking the intrinsic properties of GQDs, we identified the molecular initiating events of OH-GQDs mediated excessive inflammation in KCs, offering strategies for the de novo safe design of GQDs that target the content of oxygen-containing functional groups and the generation capacity of free radicals, which is of great significance for the development of safe, non-toxic, and efficient GQDs for clinical diagnosis and treatment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physical and chemical properties of different types of GQDs. (A) TEM images of GQDs; (B) The size distribution histograms of GQDs; (C) The maximum excitation and the maximum emission wavelength of GQDs; (D) FITR spectrum and (E) full range of XPS spectrum; (F) In vitro oxidative capacity of different types of GQDs.

The uptake and cytotoxicity profiling of different types of GQDs. (A) The representative fluorescent images of GQDs (25 μg/mL) in KCs exposed to different types of GQDs for 24 h; Red: Cell membrane labeled with Did; Blue: GQDs; Scale bar: 2.5 μm. (B) The uptake of different types of GQDs by KCs from flow cytometer and (C) quantitative analysis. The KCs were incubated with OH-GQDs, N-GQDs and NH2-GQDs at different concentrations (0, 6.25, 25 and 100 μg/mL) for 24 h, then (D) Cell viability was measured by MTT assay. (E) The content of lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) in cell supernatant and (F) the morphological changes of KCs were detected.

Transcriptomic sequencing of KCs after GQDs treatment. KCs exposed to 25 μg/mL OH-GQDs, N-GQDs, and NH2-GQD for 24 h were selected for transcriptome sequencing. (A) The number of DEGs and (B) Venn diagram performed on total transcriptome of KCs triggered by the three GQDs; (C)Top 10 GO terms regulated by all three types of GQDs; (D) Heat map of genes involved in “immunity and inflammation”, as well as “Calcium homeostasis”; (E) KEGG enrichment analysis of DEGs from different GQDs treatment groups; (F) The fold change of relative mRNA expression level of DEGs associated with inflammation and calcium homeostasis, measured by qPCR (n = 3). (G) Interaction network of DEGs in the OH-GQDs group based on the STRING database.

NLRP3 inflammasome activation participates in the occurrence of liver macrophage inflammation induced by GQDs. (A) Volcano plot of DEGs in KCs treated by OH-GQDs, key genes in the NOD-like receptor pathway, including NLRP3, IL6, NOD1, and GBP3, were all upregulated; (B) GSEA for the NOD-like receptor pathway; (C) The representative images of immunofluorescence intensity of NLRP3. KCs under different exposure conditions were incubated with anti-NLRP3 antibody for 24 h, then the cells were imaged by Confocal laser scanning microscopy, the FITC (Ex/Em=492/518 nm) and PI (Ex/Em=535/615 nm) filters were selected; Scale bar: 20 μm. (D and E) Changes in the expression of inflammatory-related proteins and (F) the elevation of cytokines in KCs exposed to different GQDs for 24 h.

Calcium homeostasis imbalance is involved in the occurrence of liver macrophage inflammation induced by GQDs. (A) The representative fluorescent images of intracellular Ca2+ in KCs exposed to different types of GQDs for 0.5, 1, 3, 6, 12 and 24 h. The intracellular Ca2+ were stained with Fluo-3 AM (Green) and nucleus with Hoechst (blue), Scale bar: 50 μm. (B) Quantitative results of average fluorescence intensity of intracellular Ca2+ using flow cytometer. (C) The immunofluorescence of NLRP3 in KCs exposed to different types of GQDs after 10 μM BAPTA-AM pretreatment, Scale bar: 20 μm. (D) The immunoblotting bands and (e) relative intensities of inflammatory-related proteins after pretreatment with 10 μM BAPTA-AM in KCs induced by different types of GQDs (25 μg/mL).

GQDs induce mitochondrial dysfunction in KCs. (A) GSEA analysis of mitochondrial-related pathways in KCs exposed to OH-GQDs. (B) The fold change of relative mRNA expression level of DEGs associated with mitochondrial function measured by qPCR (n = 3). (C) The representative fluorescent images of intramitochondrial Ca2+ in KCs were identified by using Rhod-2 AM; Scale bar: 50μm. (D) Level of MMP depolarization of KCs after treatment was analyzed by incubating cells with the JC-1 by Flow cytometer. (E) The representative fluorescent images of mtROS in KCs exposed to different types of GQDs for 24 h by using MitSOX Red; Scale bar: 50 μm. (F) The ATP production in KCs exposed to different types of GQDs for 24 h.

Intracellular calcium overload participates in mitochondrial dysfunction induced by GQDs, leading to inflammation. After pretreatment of 10 μM BAPTA-AM, (A) the representative fluorescent images of intramitochondrial Ca2+ in KCs exposed to GQDs were identified by using Rhod-2 AM; Scale bar: 50 μm; (B) Level of MMP depolarization of KCs was analyzed by incubating cells with the JC-1 by Flow cytometer;(C) The mtROS in KCs exposed to GQDs were observed by using MitSOX Red, Scale bar: 50 μm; (D) The ATP production in KCs exposed to GQDs for 24 h. After pretreating KCs with Mito-Tempo for 2 h, (E)The immunofluorescence of NLRP3 in KCs exposed to 25 μg/mL GQDs were imaged, Scale bar: 20 μm. (F) The immunoblotting bands and (G-J) relative intensities of NLRP3-related proteins were measured by western blotting.

ER calcium release is the trigger for stronger calcium homeostasis imbalance in KCs induced by OH-GQDs. (A, E) the fluorescence image of intracellular Ca2+ in KCs after pretreated with the ER Ca2+ channel inhibitor 2-APB (10 μM) for 2 h and the extracellular Ca2+ chelator EGTA (10 μM) for 1 h; (B, F) quantitative Ca2+ levels in KCs pretreated with 2-APB and EGTA were analyzed by using Fluo-3 AM (Green); (C, G)The immunoblotting bands and (D, H) relative intensities of NLRP3-related proteins were measured by western blotting.

GSH depletion induced ER stress is the molecular initiating event of OH-GQDs mediated KCs excessive inflammation. (A) Correlation analysis between oxygen-containing functional group content and in vitro oxidative capacity; (B) Matrix analysis of the correlation between oxygen-containing functional group content, in vitro oxidative capacity, and cellular response indicators; (C) ESR spectra the Hydroxyl Radical (•OH) and (D) Superoxide Radical (O2•−) generated by GQDs (50 μg·mL−1) and H2O2 (100 mM), DMPO (50 Mm) was used as a trapping agent; (E) GSH depletion caused by different types of GQDs; after incubating 190 μL of 100 μmol/L GSH in PBS with 10 μL of 25 μg/mL GQDs suspension in 37℃ for 15 min, the absorbance at 412 nm were measured after mixing with GSH-Glo reagent for 2 min; (F-H) The GSH levels、GSSG levels and the ratio of GSH/GSSG in KCs exposed to different types of GQDs for 24 h; (I) The immunoblotting bands and relative intensities of key proteins in ER stress (CHOP and GRP78) were measured by western blotting.

head.ce:title: Disruption of intracellular calcium homeostasis drives graphene quantum dots-induced inflammatory response in liver macrophages

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), with photoluminescent properties, high stability, and excellent biocompatibility, hold tremendous potential in biomedicine. It is urgent to evaluate their safety and potential health risks to promote their clinical application. Kupffer cells (KCs), as primary immune cells encountered by foreign substances entering the liver and integral to liver immunity, have yet to be systematically studied for toxicity responses to different GQDs. This study focused on three widely used GQDs (OH-GQDs, N-GQDs, and NH2-GQDs), examining their effects on KCs and elucidating the underlying mechanisms. Our findings suggested that the toxicity levels of the three GQDs on KCs are ranked as OH-GQDs > N-GQDs > NH2-GQDs, with inflammation being the main form of toxic effect, which was a consequence of GQD-induced calcium homeostasis disruption. Specifically, cytoplasmic calcium imbalance caused by GQDs leaded to mitochondrial Ca2 + overload, mitochondrial dysfunction, and mtROS generation, which subsequently activated the NLRP3 inflammasome-dependent inflammation. Crucially, we identified upstream mechanistic differences in calcium homeostasis disruption induced by each GQDs, with the most toxic OH-GQDs inducing ER stress-mediated Ca2+ release, which was closely related to the depletion of GSH caused by the generation of oxygen free radicals (•OH and O2•−). By tracing Ca2+ homeostasis, this work comprehensively mapped the upstream and downstream mechanisms of GQD-induced liver macrophage inflammation, providing new insights into the toxic effects of GQDs. Additionally, linking the intrinsic properties of GQDs, we identified the molecular initiating events of OH-GQDs mediated excessive inflammation in KCs, offering strategies for the de novo safe design of GQDs that target the content of oxygen-containing functional groups and the generation capacity of free radicals, which is of great significance for the development of safe, non-toxic, and efficient GQDs for clinical diagnosis and treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a novel class of carbon nanomaterials, exhibit tremendous potential in biomedicine due to their unique physicochemical properties and biocompatibility, particularly in applications such as bioimaging, drug delivery, and cancer therapy [1,2]. However, alongside the expanding applications of GQDs, their potential risks have also garnered significant attention. Although GQDs differ from traditional heavy-metal-based quantum dots (QDs), being primarily composed of carbon and thus considered biocompatible, numerous studies have shown that GQDs can accumulate in various organs and induce multiple toxic effects including apoptosis, ferroptosis, inflammation, and DNA damage [3–5]. Most studies to date focus on a specific type of GQD, leading to data discrepancies across studies due to differences in quantum dot types, cell lines, and exposure methods. Therefore, evaluating the safety of various GQD types and identifying specific GQDs with higher biocompatibility for biomedical applications is an essential step toward their clinical translation.

It is estimated that 30–99 % of nanomaterials (NMs) accumulate in the liver after entering the body. The presence of NMs in the liver have been shown to interfere with hepatic metabolism and homeostasis [6]. Numerous studies report the adverse effects of NMs on the liver, including metallic NMs-induced liver disorders, CNTs-induced hepatic steatosis and liver injury, Graphene-induced hepatic dysfunction [7–9]. However, as a unique and crucial immune organ, the liver plays a key role in immune defense and regulation, and few studies have focused on the impact of GQDs on hepatic immune [10,11]. Liver macrophages, particularly Kupffer cells (KCs), hold a pivotal position in hepatic immune, participating in both innate and adaptive immune responses in the liver, being essential regulators in liver immunity and disease progression. It is reported that NMs are first recognized and phagocytosed by KCs upon entering the liver via the bloodstream, making KCs the primary focus of NMs-liver interaction studies [6,12].

Previous research has indicated that inflammation is one of the main toxic responses in macrophages following QDs exposure. For example, MPA-CdTe QDs induce IL-1β secretion in monocyte macrophages (BV2 cells) through TLR2/MyD88/NF-kappa B signaling and NLRP3 inflammasome activation [13]. Similarly, InP/ZnS QDs cause inflammation in mouse bone marrow-derived macrophages (BMMs) by increasing ROS levels [14]. However, in the case of GQDs, which have a chemical composition distinct from traditional heavy-metal-containing QDs, it remains to be confirmed whether inflammation is a primary effect in GQDs-induced KCs damage. In addition to oxidative stress, which is commonly mentioned mechanism, the upstream molecular mechanisms of GQD-induced KC inflammation and subcellular damage require further investigation. Thus, this study employs RNA sequencing (RNA-Seq), a powerful tool for identifying key genes and pathways in disease research, to explore the characteristics and mechanisms of GQDs-induced toxicity in KCs.

Moreover, the physicochemical properties of QDs influence their toxicity. Xie et al. demonstrated that different functional groups of GQDs differentially affect autophagy induction, with OH-GQDs causing autophagic flux blockage and subsequent cell death, while NH2-GQDs and COOH-GQDs lead autophagic survival [15]. The study of Zhang found that the pro-inflammatory effects of different functionalized PEG-pQDs were closely related to the terminal functional groups on the PEG chains (-COOH, -NH2, -OH, and -OCH3), with COOH-PEG-pQDs causing the most severe pro-inflammatory effects in human lung epithelial cells and macrophages [16]. These findings underscore the critical role of terminal functional groups in QD toxicity. As toxicologists facing a range of GQDs with different functional groups, our ultimate goal is to correlate the physicochemical properties of various GQDs with their toxicities, based on toxicity evaluations and mechanistic explorations. We aim to elucidate the origins of GQD-induced toxicity, including the intrinsic properties of the materials and biologically relevant molecular initiating events.

In summary, this study primarily investigates toxic effects of three types of GQDs with different surface functional groups, using KCs as an in vitro model of liver macrophages. Based on the comparison of the toxicity of different GQDs in KCs, we leverage transcriptomic analysis to elucidate the mechanisms and sources of GQD-induced toxicity, aiming to clarify the sources of toxicity for certain GQDs, to dissect the key properties closely related to toxicity, so as to provide scientific guidance for selecting GQD types and doses in biomedical applications and for safe-by-design of GQDs.

The OH-GQDs (XF091), N-GQDs (XF241) and NH2-GQDs (XF229) were purchased from the Xianfeng Nano Co., LTD (Nanjing, China). Different GQDs working fluids (25 μg/mL) were dripped onto the copper mesh, then observed the morphologies by TEM (JEM-2100, JEOL Ltd) after drying, and the particle size was measured using Image J 7.0. The hydrated particle size and zeta potential of GQDs were detected using a Mastersizer (Zetasizer-Nano-ZS90, Malvern Instruments, UK). The FTIR (Thermo Nicolet iS5) and the XPS (Thermo Kalpha) were employed to analyze the surface chemical states and functional groups of GQDs. The fluorescence spectrum of GQDs is obtained by fluorescence spectrophotometer from Hitachi Japan (F-4600). The in vitro oxidation capacity of GQDs were determined by 2’,7’-Dichlorofluorescin diacetate from Aladdin Co., LTD (Shanghai, China).

The mouse Kupffer cells, KUP5, was procured from the RIKEN Cell Bank. The Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10 % (v/v) fetal bovine serum (BI, USA), 10 μg/mL bovine insulin, 250 μM 1-thioglycerol (Sigma, USA), 10 kU/mL penicillin, 10 mg/mL streptomycin and 5 mg/mL gentamicin (Solarbio, Beijing, China) was used as the medium for KUP5.

Immunofluorescence and flow cytometry were used to detect the uptake of GQDs by KCs. The KCs in the logarithmic growth phase were seeded with a density of 1 × 105 cells per well. After overnight, 25 μg/mL OH-GQDs, N-GQDs and NH2-GQDs were added for 24 h exposure. Then the fluorescence of KCs was observed by Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) (FV1000, Olympus, Japan). To quantify the uptake of GQDs by KCs, flow cytometry (FACSCANTO II, BD Bioscience, USA) was used to detect the side scatter (SSC) of KCs under the same treatment.

The KCs were seeded at a certain density (1 ×104 cells per well for 96-well plate, 2 ×105 cells per well for 12-well plate) and cultured until adherent. OH-GQDs, N-GQDs and NH2-GQDs at different concentrations (0, 6.25, 25 and 100 μg/mL) were added, respectively. After 24 h exposure, the cell viability of KCs was detected by MTT assay (Meilunbio, China), the LDH release was detected using kit (Beyotime, China), and morphological changes were observed by microscopy (ZEISS AG, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transcriptomic analysis of KCs after GQDs exposure
ce:section.ce:para: According to cytotoxicity results, KCs exposed to 25 μg/mL OH-GQDs, N-GQDs, and NH2-GQD for 24 h were selected for transcriptome sequencing. The RNA-seq was conducted by Genedenovo Biotechnology Co., LTD. (Guangzhou, China). The total RNA from different treated KCs were extracted and qualified to ensure sequencing accuracy. Then the mRNA with length of 200–300 base pairs was used as template to synthesize and purify double-stranded cDNA, and the sequencing library was constructed by PCR amplification based on cDNA. After qualified, the library was sequenced using the Illumina NextSeq500 platform (Illumina. San Diego, CA, USA). Using Ensembl_release104 as the reference genome, the sample data were performed bioinformatics analyses for differentially expressed genes (DEGs) identification, GO function enrichment, KEGG pathway enrichment and GSEA enrichment.

To ensure the accuracy of sequencing, key DEGs were simultaneously detected by RT-qPCR. Total RNA obtained from DCs using RNA-easy Isolation Reagent (Vazyme, Nanjing, China) was inverse transcribed into cDNA using HiScript II Q RT SuperMix for qPCR (Vazyme, Nanjing, China). RT-qPCR was carried out using AceQ qPCR SYBR Green Master Mix (Vazyme, Nanjing, China) on the Quant Studio™ 7 Real-Time PCR System (Life Technologies, MA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of inflammatory cytokines by ELISA
ce:section.ce:para: KCs in the logarithmic growth phase were seeded in 6-well plates at a density of 4 × 105 per well, then the adherent cells were exposed to OH-GQDs, N-GQDs, and NH2-GQDs at different concentrations (0, 6.25, and 25 μg/mL). After 24 h exposure, the level of multiple cytokines (IL-6, IL-18, IL-1β and TNF-α) in the cell supernatant was detected by Elisa kit from Ruixinbio (Quanzhou, China).

KCs seeding in Laser confocal dish were treated with OH-GQDs, N-GQDs, and NH2-GQDs at different concentrations (0, 6.25, and 25 μg/mL) for 24 h. Then KCs under different exposure conditions were fixed through 4 % paraformaldehyde (Beyotime, China) and permeabilized using 0.3 % Triton 100 (Beyotime, China), subsequently incubated with anti-NLRP3 antibody (1:200, A24294, Abclonal) for 16 h on the basis of Immunol Staining Blocking (Beyotime, China). FITC-conjugated Goat Anti-Rabbit IgG (1:500, AS011, Abclonal) were used as secondary antibodies. The NLRP3 of KCs were observed by CLSM (FV1000, Olympus, Japan) after staining nucleus with Antifade Mounting Medium with PI (Beyotime, China).

KCs treating with OH-GQDs, N-GQDs, and NH2-GQDs at different concentrations (0, 6.25, and 25 μg/mL) for 24 h were lysed using ice cold cell lysis buffer with 1 mM PMSF after washing. After quantifing using the BCA kit from EpiZyme (Shanghai, China), the immunoblotting was conducted following the standard method with 20–40 μg denatured proteins. The PVDF membrane was blocked and incubated with primary antibodies including anti-NLRP3 (ABclonal, China), anti-IL-1β (Abcam, UK), anti-caspase-1(Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), anti-pro-caspase-1(Abcam, UK), anti-CHOP (HuaBio, China), anti-GRP78(Affinity Bioscsience, China) and anti-GAPDH (Proteintech, China) overnight at 4 °C. Tagging was performed using with anti-mouse/rabbit secondary antibodies from Proteintech (Wuhan, China). Fluorescence signals were detected after incubating with ECL Detection Reagent (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

The levels of intracellular Ca2+ in KCs were demonstrated using immunofluorescence staining by Fluo-3 AM probe (Beyotime, China). Specifically, the adherent KCs were exposed to 25 μg/mL OH-GQDs, N-GQDs, and NH2-GQDs for 0 h, 0.5 h, 1 h, 3 h, 6 h, 12 h and 24 h, respectively. After removal of the medium with GQDs, intracellular Ca2+ were labeled with 5 μM Fluo-3 AM probe and incubated at 37 °C in the dark for 30 min, followed by dye removal, rinsing three times and incubation at 37 °C in the dark for another 10 min. Finally, intracellular Ca2+ levels were observed by fluorescence microscopy (ZEISS AG, Germany) and quantified by flow cytometry (FACSCANTO II, BD Bioscience, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calcium overload and dysfunction of mitochondria
ce:section.ce:para: The Ca2+ overload within mitochondria was indicated by Rhod-2 AM probe (Yeasen, Shanghai, China). After exposure to different GQDs for 24 h, KCs were incubated with 2.5 μM Rhod-2 AM probe working solution for 30 min, then the dye were removed and washed three times. The KCs were observed and photographed by fluorescence microscope (ZEISS AG, Germany). Assessment of mitochondrial dysfunction was performed through mitochondrial ROS (mtROS), mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) and ATP production. The mtROS, which were characterized by MitoSOXRed (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), were observed by fluorescence microscopy (ZEISS AG, Germany). MMPs were monitored by quantifying the fluorescence emissions from mitochondrial JC-1 monomers or aggregates using flow cytometry (FACSCANTO II, BD Bioscience, USA) and CLSM (FV1000, Olympus, Japan) at 488 nm/525 nm. ATP production was assessed using the ATP kit (Beyotime, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH depletion in abiotic/biotic systems
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of three GQDs to deplete GSH under abiotic conditions was measured by the GSH detection kit from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. To each 1.5 mL Capacity Microcentrifuge Tubes we added 190 μL of 100 μmol/L GSH in PBS and 10 μL of 25 μg/mL GQDs suspension, followed by 15 min incubation in 37℃ Shaking table. Then 20 μL of each tube was transferred to a 96-well plate, and mixed with 180 μL of GSH-Glo reagent for 2 min incubation at room temperature. According to the manufacturer’s instructions, the absorbance at 412 nm of the solution in each well was recorded for calculation of GSH levels. GSH and GSSG levels in KCs after different GQDs exposure were detected by corresponding kits (Beyotime, China). The procedure for treating KCs using GQDs was the same as described above. After collection of KCs, three times the volume of cell precipitation was added to the protein removal reagent and fully Vortex, and then the samples were subjected to two rapid freezing-thawing using liquid nitrogen. It was allowed to stand for 5 min at 4 °C and then centrifuged at 10,000 g for 10 min. The supernatant was used for subsequent assays, which were performed according to the manufacturer’s instructions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of hydroxyl radical (•OH) and superoxide radical (O2•−)
ce:section.ce:para: By using 50 mM DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide, MedChemExpress) as the spin trapping agent, the Hydroxyl Radical (•OH) and Superoxide Radical (O2•−) generation of GQDs was detected in a mixture of H2O2 (100 mM) and GQDs (50 μg·mL−1) by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR).

All data were expressed as mean ± SD. All values were obtained from at least three independent experiments. Statistical significance was evaluated using two-tailed heteroscedastic Student’s t-tests. The significant difference between groups was considered statistically significant when the p value was lower than 0.05.

The bio-effects of nanomaterials are closely associated with their inherent properties. Consequently, prior to conducting toxicity studies, this research systematically characterized the intrinsic properties of GQDs utilizing a suite of analytical techniques. TEM was employed to observe the morphology of the GQDs ( Fig. 1A), revealing that all three types of GQDs were uniformly dispersed with a round and orderly crystalline structure. The particle size of OH-GQDs, N-GQDs, and NH2-GQDs were primarily distributed between 0.6 nm and 6.6 nm, with average sizes of 3.13 ± 1.27 nm, 1.47 ± 0.97 nm, and 1.49 ± 0.89 nm, respectively (Fig. 1B). Table 1 presents the hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of GQDs in various dispersing media. The hydrodynamic diameters of GQDs were significantly larger than their original sizes, likely due to slight aggregation of the GQDs and the formation of a “protein corona”[17]. The zeta potentials of OH-GQDs, N-GQDs, and NH2-GQDs in DMEM complete medium respectively were −10.50 ± 1.45 mV, −8.30 ± 1.22 mV, and −7.88 ± 0.92 mV, suggesting good dispersibility of GQDs. Fluorescence spectroscopy, a fundamental characteristic of quantum dots, was further examined. As shown in Fig. 1C, the maximum fluorescence emission wavelengths ranged from 340 to 390 nm, and the maximum excitation wavelengths ranged from 460 to 480 nm, indicating that all three GQDs exhibit blue fluorescence. At the same concentration, the fluorescence intensities of the three GQDs were in the order of N-GQDs > OH-GQDs > NH2-GQDs. FTIR and XPS were combined to analyze the functional groups of the three GQDs. All three GQDs exhibited a characteristic peak corresponding to CC at 1620 cm-1. Additionally, the FTIR spectrum of OH-GQDs also included an absorption peak near 1000 cm-1 corresponding to O−H in-plane bending, and a peak at 3000 cm-1 corresponding to O−H stretching, indicating the presence of hydroxyl functional groups and carboxyl groups. The FTIR spectrum of N-GQDs showed characteristic peaks of the reactant functional groups during the nitrogen doping process, such as the peak (1500–1600 cm-1) corresponding to N − H in-plane bending. The FTIR spectrum of NH2-GQDs revealed an absorption peak at 650 cm-1 corresponding to N − H out-of-plane bending, suggesting the presence of amino groups. The XPS survey spectra and deconvoluted spectra of GQDs are shown in Fig. 1E and Figure S1, respectively. The O/C ratios of OH-GQDs, N-GQDs, and NH2-GQDs were 90.07 %, 57.68 %, and 51.99 %, respectively ( Table 2), indicating that the content of oxygen-containing functional groups in the three GQDs decreased in the order of OH-GQDs > N-GQDs > NH2-GQDs. Accordingly, we found that OH-GQDs with the highest content of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface had the highest in vitro oxidation capacity (Fig. 1F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uptake of GQDs by liver macrophages and its cytotoxicity
ce:section.ce:para: Upon entering the liver, nanoparticles are initially recognized and internalized by liver macrophages leading to various outcomes. This study employs mouse liver macrophages (KCs) as an in vitro cellular model to investigate the cellular uptake and safety assessment of three types of GQDs with potential biomedical applications. Leveraging the intrinsic fluorescence of GQDs, the distribution of blue fluorescence within KCs was used to directly characterize their uptake of GQDs. As shown in Fig. 2A, blue fluorescence appeared in KCs after 25 μg/mL GQDs exposure, indicating uptake of all three types of GQDs. Among them, OH-GQDs and N-GQDs were closely distributed around the cell membrane, while NH2-GQDs exhibited a more dispersed distribution. Further quantitative analysis of uptake was achieved using flow cytometry, revealing no difference in the uptake of the three types of GQDs (25 μg/mL) by KCs (Fig. 2B and C). Subsequently, the cytotoxicity of GQDs to KCs were characterized through cell viability, cell membrane damage and cellular morphology. The cell viability results are depicted in Fig. 2D, showing a concentration-dependent decline in the viability of KCs following exposure to each type of GQDs across a concentration range of 6.25–100 μg/mL. At the same concentrations, OH-GQDs induced the most severe reduction in cell viability, with a viability of only 26.24 % at a concentration of 100 μg/mL. The level of LDH in the culture medium (Fig. 2E), which reflects the degree of cell membrane damage, found that when the concentration was greater than 25 μg/mL, OH-GQDs and N-GQDs significantly increased the LDH level in the culture supernatant of KCs (P < 0.05), and the level of LDH in OH-GQDs group was higher than that in N-GQDs group, while NH2-GQDs did not cause membrane damage of KCs. Fig. 2F illustrates the cell morphology of KCs after GQDs exposure. After treatment with OH-GQDs, the number of cells gradually decreased with the increase of dose. When the concentration of OH-GQDs increased to 25 μg/mL, the increasing cell antenna, cell swelling and cell membrane damage were also observed. However, for N-GQDs and NH2-GQDs, a significant reduction in cells number and a small amount of cell swelling were observed only when the exposure dose was 100 μg/mL. All these results suggest that OH-GQDs were the most cytotoxic of the three GQDs, followed by N-GQDs and NH2-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transcriptomic sequencing of KCs after three types of GQDs treatment
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the underlying mechanism of the difference in cytotoxicity of three kinds of GQDs, we analyzed the transcriptional expression profiles of KCs after exposure to GQDs. Principal components analysis (Figure. S2a) showed good within-group reproducibility and significant differences among the four groups, which ensured the reliability of the sequencing. We further analyzed the difference between the transcripts of KCs exposed to GQDs and untreated KCs. As shown in Fig. 3A, compared with the control group, 94 genes were significantly changed in the OH-GQDs exposure group, which called differentially expressed genes (DEGs), including 63 up-regulated genes and 31 down-regulated genes, whereas the N-GQDs and NH2-GQDs groups identified 72 and 69 DEGs, respectively. The Venn diagram of DEGs (Fig. 3B) showed that 15 genes, including Col6a3, Fbln2, Bgn, Sparc, Ankrd1, Epb41l3 and Pxdn, were simultaneously regulated by all three GQDs, which were involved in biological processes such as extracellular matrix components, cell signaling, cell adhesion, migration and proliferation. We further performed GO functional enrichment for changed genes in the three GQDs exposure groups. Fig. 3C showed the top 10 GO terms regulated by three types of GQDs, including "cellular anatomical entity", "binding", "cellular process", "response to stimulus" and "metabolic process". Intriguingly, the most toxic OH-GQDs cause the most gene expression abnormalities in every GO term. In addition, we sought to find the differences in gene changes induced by the three GQDs and analyzed the top 30 DEGs in each group (Figure S2B). Among the top 30 DEGs in the OH-GQDs group, a substantial number of genes related to calcium homeostasis regulation and inflammation modulation were upregulated. The analysis of the immune-inflammatory and calcium homeostasis-related gene profiles (Fig. 3D), along with the validation of key genes by RT-qPCR (Fig. 3F), further provides compelling evidence for the correlation between OH-GQDs exposure and the upregulation of inflammatory genes (NLRP3, IL-1β, IL-6, IL-18, Thbs1, and Itgax) and genes associated with calcium imbalance (Sphk1, Pde1a, Stim1, Orai1, Lpar6, Psap, Met, and ATP2c1). In contrast, N-GQDs and NH2-GQDs only led to a slight upregulation of a subset of genes (Sphk1 and Pde1a; NLRP3, IL-1β, and Thbs1).

Additionally, this study continued to explore the alterations in signal pathways of KCs caused by GQDs exposure through KEGG enrichment pathway analysis (Fig. 3E). The most significantly enriched pathway identified in the OH-GQDs group was the “NOD-like receptor signaling pathway” (Q < 0.05), in which NLRP3, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-18 were significantly upregulated, suggesting that OH-GQDs induce NLRP3 inflammasome-dependent inflammatory responses. No significant perturbation of this pathway was observed following exposure to the other two GQDs. Furthermore, we constructed an interaction network of DEGs in the OH-GQDs group based on the STRING database to elucidate the key targets of OH-GQD. From the interaction network of DEGs (Fig. 3G), we found that the Cask, Calhm6, IRF1, and Casr interacted closely with multiple other genes after OH-GQDs exposure. Specifically, calcium/calmodulin-dependent serine protein kinase (CASK) is a member of the membrane-associated guanylate kinase superfamily, involved in regulating cell proliferation and cytoskeletal remodeling; the protein encoded by the CASR gene is a plasma membrane G-protein coupled receptor that senses subtle changes in circulating calcium concentration, playing a crucial role in maintaining calcium ion homeostasis; CALHM6, uniquely highly expressed in immune cells, is a member of the calcium homeostasis regulatory family, and like the transcription factor IRF1, is also associated with macrophage pathogen response and pro-inflammatory stimuli. The above result of pathway and key target genes point to the possibility that the cytotoxic effects of OH-GQDs may involve inflammation mediated by NOD-like receptors as well as an imbalance in calcium homeostasis in KCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs induce NLRP3 inflammasome-dependent inflammatory in Kupffer Cells
ce:section.ce:para: As one of the key mechanisms indicated by the previous transcriptome sequencing results, the NOD-like receptor (NLR) pathway and its mediated inflammatory response were further explored. Fig. 4A presents the volcano plot of differentially expressed genes for OH-GQDs, from which it can be observed that key genes in the NOD-like receptor pathway, including NLRP3, IL6, NOD1, and GBP3, were all upregulated. Concurrently, the Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) for the NOD-like receptor pathway (Fig. 4B) yielded an enrichment score (ES) of 0.565, signifying a clear activation of this pathway.

As a member of the NOD-like receptor family, NOD-like receptor protein-3 (NLRP3) is typically activated when cells are stimulated by exogenous substances, forming NLRP3 inflammasomes and leading to the release of inflammatory cytokines. Therefore, we further examined NLRP3 inflammasome activation, related protein expression and cytokine secretion in KCs exposed to different GQDs (6.25 and 25 μg/mL) for 24 h at the translational level. By comparing the changes in NLRP3 within KCs induced by the three types of GQDs at the same concentration, it was found that OH-GQDs caused a higher activation of NLRP3 in KCs than N-GQDs, while NH2-GQDs had no significant effect on NLRP3 in KCs (Fig. 4C). Additionally, we quantified the expression of NLRP3 and related inflammatory proteins by immunoblotting, as shown in Fig. 4D. At a concentration of 6.25 μg/mL, both OH-GQDs and N-GQDs led to the activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome pathway in KUP 5 cells, characterized by a significant increase in the protein expression of NLRP3, Caspase-1, and IL-1β, and a significant decrease in the protein expression of Pro-caspase-1 (P < 0.05). These changes were observed only at a concentration of 25 μg/mL in the NH2-GQDs group. Furthermore, we examined the level of key pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β, IL-6, IL-18, and TNF-α in the supernatant of KCs after 24 h exposure to different GQDs (6.25 and 25 μg/mL), as shown in Fig. 4E. Consistent with the trend of NLRP3 inflammasome activation, we found that among the three materials, OH-GQDs induced the highest secretion of inflammatory cytokines in KCs, followed by N-GQDs, and NH2-GQDs had the least effect.

To reverse verify the key role of NLRP3 in GQD-induced inflammatory response in KCs, the NLRP3 inhibitor (MCC 950) was used to pretreat KCs for 2 hours. There was no difference in the expression of NLRP3, Caspase-1, IL-1β and Pro-caspase-1 proteins between the MCC 950 pretreatment group and the control group, indicating that inhibiting NLRP3 activation helped to maintain the normal expression of inflammation-related proteins in KCs under GQD stimulation (Figure S3). All of the above indicated that OH-GQDs, N-GQDs and NH2-GQDs caused NLRP3 inflammasome-dependent inflammatory in KCs, among which OH-GQDs had the most pronounced inflammation induction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The imbalance of calcium homeostasis involved in the inflammation of KCs induced by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to inflammation, imbalance in calcium homeostasis was another mechanism identified by transcriptome sequencing. In this study, Ca2+ green fluorescent probe was used to detect the changes of cytosolic Ca2+ in KCs after exposure to different types of GQDs (25 μg/mL) for various durations (0 h, 0.5 h, 1 h, 3 h, 6 h, 12 h, and 24 h), so as to explore the imbalance of calcium homeostasis mediated by GQDs. Fig. 5A and B respectively present the intracellular Ca2+ fluorescence images and the average Ca2+ fluorescence intensity under different treatment conditions. Both fluorescence and flow cytometry results indicated that all three types of GQDs induced an increase in cytosolic Ca2+ after 3 hours of exposure. The increase in Ca2+ caused by N-GQDs and NH2-GQDs reached a peak at 6 hours, then declined until 24 hours. However, after exposure to OH-GQDs, the Ca2+ level in KCs continuously increased from 3 to 12 hours, and at any time point, the cytosolic Ca2+ in KCs exposed to OH-GQDs was greater than that of the other two groups.

Notably, among the three materials, OH-GQDs, which could induce the most severe calcium homeostasis imbalance, caused the most intense inflammation in KCs, suggesting a possible regulatory relationship between the two mechanisms. To elucidate the relationship between the GQDs-mediated calcium homeostasis imbalance and NLRP3 inflammasome-dependent inflammation in KCs, the Ca2+ chelator BAPTA-AM (10 μM) was co-incubated with KCs and GQDs. After 24 hours, the expression of NLRP3 immunofluorescence in KCs exposed to the three types of GQDs was examined, and it was found that treatment with BAPTA-AM significantly alleviated GQDs-induced NLRP3 activation (Fig. 5C). The expression of proteins NLRP3, Caspase-1, Pro-caspase-1, and IL-1β also suggested that BAPTA-AM helped maintain the normal expression of inflammation-related proteins in KCs under GQDs exposure (Fig. 5D). The above results revealed that the disruption of calcium homeostasis caused by cytosolic Ca2+ overload might be an upstream mechanism of NLRP3 inflammasome-dependent inflammatory response in KCs induced by GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calcium overload-mediated mitochondrial dysfunction involved in inflammations of KCs induced by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Previous experiments have demonstrated that GQDs can induce inflammation in KCs through calcium homeostasis imbalance, though the specific regulatory mechanisms between the two remain unclear. Numerous studies have identified mitochondria as a key link between calcium homeostasis and inflammation. When the cytoplasm is exposed to a high-calcium environment, mitochondria absorb intracellular calcium. While the absorption of calcium within a certain range promotes cell survival, excessive calcium uptake drives cells toward an inflammatory state or even death. Therefore, we explored mitochondrial calcium levels and mitochondrial function in KCs stimulated by GQDs.

First, a GSEA analysis based on RNA-seq was conducted to identify disturbances in mitochondrial-related pathways at the transcriptional level. As shown in Fig. 6A, more than ten mitochondrial-related pathways, including “mitochondrial translation”, “mitochondrial gene expression”, “mitochondrial large ribosomal subunit” and “extrinsic component of mitochondrial inner membrane” were perturbed under the stimulation of OH-GQDs. Subsequent analysis of key genes also revealed abnormal expression of several mitochondrial-related genes under GQDs stimulation. These include the mitochondrial carbon metabolism enzyme-coding gene SHMT2, the mitochondrial ATP-dependent protease-coding gene Lonp1, the mitochondrial ribosomal protein-coding gene MRPS18B, and other mitochondrial protein-coding genes such as ALDH2 and MDH2. The OH-GQDs had the most significant effect on the five genes mentioned above (Fig. 6B). These results provide transcriptional-level evidence that GQDs induce mitochondrial dysfunction in KCs.

At the translational level, given the previous findings on calcium homeostasis imbalance, we prioritized the detection of changes in mitochondrial Ca2+ levels in KCs following GQDs exposure (Fig. 6C). The results indicated that all three types of GQDs (25 μg/mL) led to an increase in mitochondrial Ca2+ level in KCs. At the same concentration, OH-GQDs caused the most significant increase in mitochondrial Ca2+ level compared to N-GQDs and NH2-GQDs. MMP is a key parameter reflecting the functional state of mitochondria. As shown in Figure S4, after 24 hours of GQDs stimulation, MMP depolarization were observed in KCs. OH-GQDs induced MMP depolarization at a low concentration (6.25 μg/mL), as evidenced by a decrease in red JC-1 aggregates and an increase in green JC-1 monomers. Flow cytometry quantification of MMP depolarization levels revealed that N-GQDs caused MMP depolarization only when the exposure concentration reached 25 μg/mL (Fig. 6D). The mtROS and ATP production, key indicators of oxidative stress and respiratory function, were also assessed. As shown in Fig. 6E, only OH-GQDs caused a significant increase of mtROS in KCs at low concentrations, consistent with previous trends. As for ATP, the results indicated that at a concentration of 6.25 μg/mL, OH-GQDs reduced ATP levels in KCs to 95.22 % (P < 0.05), while the other two materials did not affect ATP production in KCs until the concentration increased to 25 μg/mL (Fig. 6F).

To further confirm whether mitochondrial dysfunction are key intermediate links in GQDs-mediated calcium homeostasis imbalance that induces inflammation in KCs, we first employed the intracellular Ca2+ chelator BAPTM-AM. After chelating excess cytosolic Ca2+ with BAPTM-AM, we measured mitochondrial Ca2+ level and found that the mitochondrial calcium overload in KCs caused by OH-GQDs, N-GQDs, and NH2-GQDs was reversed ( Fig. 7A). At the same time, BAPTM-AM further restored the MMP depolarization (Figs. 7B and S5), the increase in mtROS (Fig. 7C), and the reduction in ATP production (Fig. 7D) caused by OH-GQDs, N-GQDs, and NH2-GQDs in KCs. These findings suggest that GQDs-mediated cytosolic Ca2+ overload is absorbed by the mitochondria, leading to mitochondrial calcium overload and mitochondrial dysfunction.

As one of the key outcomes of mitochondrial dysfunction, an increase in mtROS, is a direct trigger for NLRP3 inflammasome activation. Therefore, we further pretreated KCs with the mtROS scavenger MitoTempo to confirm the regulatory relationship between mitochondrial dysfunction and inflammation. The results showed that after MitoTempo cleared the accumulation of mtROS induced by GQDs in KCs, the activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome in each group was correspondingly reduced, especially in the OH-GQDs exposure group (Fig. 7E). Additionally, the increase in NLRP3, Caspase-1, and IL-1β protein expression and the decrease in Pro-caspase-1 protein expression in KCs were suppressed after MitoTempo pretreatment (Fig. 7F), indicating that GQDs induce NLRP3 inflammasome-dependent inflammation through the accumulation of mtROS. Together, these results provide strong evidence that mitochondrial calcium overload and dysfunction are key intermediates in GQDs-mediated calcium homeostasis imbalance, which leads to inflammation in KCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ER calcium release is the trigger for stronger calcium homeostasis imbalance in KCs induced by OH-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: It has been discovered that calcium homeostasis imbalance in KCs is upstream of mitochondrial dysfunction, inflammation, and other toxic cascades induced by GQDs. However, where did the cytosolic burst of calcium come from? Studies suggest that endoplasmic reticulum (ER) Ca2+ release and extracellular Ca2+ influx are two major sources of cytosolic Ca2+.

To determine whether the increased Ca2+ come from the ER, we pretreated KCs with the ER Ca2+ channel inhibitor 2-APB (10 μM) for 2 hours, followed by exposure to 25 μg/mL GQDs. As shown in Figs. 8A and B, 2-APB partially restored the increase of cytosolic Ca2+ induced by OH-GQDs in KCs, while KCs in the N-GQDs and NH2-GQDs groups were insensitive to 2-APB. Additionally, by measuring the expression of the NLRP3 inflammasome and related inflammatory proteins, we found that 2-APB significantly restored the elevated NLRP3 immunofluorescence (Figure S6A), as well as the increased expression of NLRP3, Caspase-1, and IL-1β proteins and the decreased expression of Pro-caspase-1 proteins following OH-GQDs exposure in KCs (Fig. 8C and D). These results suggest that the increase in cytosolic Ca2+ induced by OH-GQDs partially originates from the ER, while the cytosolic Ca2+ increase caused by N-GQDs and NH2-GQDs is unrelated to the ER.

Then, we also investigated the role of extracellular Ca2+ in GQDs-induced calcium homeostasis imbalance in KCs. Fig. 8E and F showed the fluorescence images and quantitative analysis of cytosolic Ca2+ in KCs exposured to 25 μg/mL GQDs after pretreatment with the extracellular Ca2+ chelator EGTA for 1 h. The results indicated that chelating extracellular Ca2+ partially restored the calcium overload mediated by OH-GQDs in KCs, and completely reversed the calcium overload mediated by N-GQDs and NH2-GQDs. Correspondingly, the subsequent activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome in KCs induced by GQDs (Figure S6B) and the abnormal expression of NLRP3, Caspase-1, IL-1β, and Pro-caspase-1 proteins (Fig. 8G and H) were suppressed to varying degrees by EGTA, along with the recovery of calcium homeostasis imbalance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GSH depletion induced ER stress is the molecular initiating event of OH-GQDs mediated KCs excessive inflammation
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate why only OH-GQDs, rather than all three GQDs, trigger ER calcium release, correlation analysis was performed between intrinsic properties of the three GQDs and important intracellular toxic end points. As shown in Fig. 9A, the content of oxygen-containing functional groups showed a high correlation with the in vitro oxidation capacity, R= 0.96, suggesting that the high in vitro oxidation capacity of OH-GQDs was the result of large amount of oxygen-containing functional groups. Correlation analysis with further inclusion of cellular effector indicators (Fig. 9B), shown that the content of oxygen-containing functional groups is negatively correlated with cell survival rate and ATP production. However, it was positively correlated with the production of mtROS, Ca2+ content, the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α and IL-18) and the expression of inflammation-related proteins (NLRP3, Caspase-1 and IL-1β), indicating that the high content of oxygen-containing functional groups and oxidative capacity were the key factors mediating the pro-inflammatory effect of OH-GQDs, which distinguished them from the other two GQDs. High in vitro oxidative capacity is often accompanied by a unique ability to produce large amounts of free radicals. Using electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), we confirmed that OH-GQDs can produce •OH, with a characteristic spectrum displaying four peaks with a 1:2:2:1 intensity ratio from left to right (Fig. 9 C), and O2•−, showing a six-peak pattern, with four main peaks of roughly equal height at positions 1, 2, 4, and 6 and two smaller peaks at positions 3 and 5 (Fig. 9D). Studies suggest that the oxidative environment within the ER is maintained by the GSH/GSSG ratio, which promotes protein folding; any disruption in this ratio can lead to ER stress and calcium release from the ER. We hypothesized that the high levels of •OH and O2•− generated by OH-GQDs might lead to GSH depletion and ER stress. Furthermore, GSH consumption by GQDs in abiotic and biotic systems and ER stress in KCs after GQDs exposure were examined. As shown in Fig. 9E, OH-GQDs significantly consumed GSH in the non-biological system, likely due to their production of •OH and O2•−. In biotic systems, while all three types of GQDs at 25 μg/mL impacted GSH and GSSG levels, only the KCs in the OH-GQDs group exhibited a marked imbalance in the GSH/GSSG ratio, distinct from the reversible stress responses seen with the other two GQDs (Fig. 9F-H). Finally, the expression levels of CHOP and GRP78, key proteins of endoplasmic reticulum stress, in KCs exposed to GQDs are shown in Fig. 9I, 25 μg/mL OH-GQDs caused significant upregulation of CHOP and GRP78, unlike the other two GQDs at the same concentration. These results suggested that the extensive free radical production by OH-GQDs and the resulting GSH/GSSG imbalance might be the molecular initiating event for the more intense inflammatory response in KCs compared to the other GQDs.

Although GQDs are free from toxic heavy-metal elements in their chemical composition, recent in vivo studies indicate that GQDs can cause damage to the liver, kidneys, and nervous system, showing they are not as inherently “safe” as once anticipated [18,19]. Thus, comprehensive toxicological safety assessments remain a critical step for GQDs to progress from laboratory research to clinical application. With its abundant blood flow and developed reticuloendothelial system, the liver is one of the primary organs where GQDs distribute and accumulate. KCs are among the most important immune cells in the liver and are also some of the first immune cells to encounter foreign substances entering the liver. To understand the interaction between GQDs and key immune cells in the liver, this study used KCs as an in vitro cell model to investigate the differences in liver macrophage damage induced by three types of GQDs, clarify their potential molecular mechanisms, and identify toxicity sources based on the physicochemical properties of GQDs.

Initially, we characterized the physicochemical properties of the three GQDs, including primary size, hydrated particle size, zeta potential, fluorescence characteristics, oxygen-containing functional group content, and in vitro oxidative capacity, aiming to establish connections between these properties and subsequent toxic effects. In terms of size, the primary and hydrated particle sizes of the three GQDs in this study are all less than 10 nm. Wang et al. summarized the toxicity of GQDs by size classification and found that the impact of various GQDs with size distributions within 10 nm on cell viability does not vary significantly [20], allowing us to exclude size differences as a major factor in cytotoxicity in this study. Similarly, all three types of GQDs are negatively charged in DMEM medium, with no substantial differences in absolute values of zeta potential. In carbon-based nanomaterials such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., -OH, -COOH, CO, -O-) are chemically bonded to the carbon lattice, typically introduced during synthesis or post-oxidation processes, and are crucial for tuning the physicochemical and biological properties of GQDs. In our study, OH-GQDs are characterized by a high density of hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxyl (-COOH) groups on their surface, as confirmed by FTIR and XPS analyses. This correspondingly increased the in vitro oxidation capacity of OH-GQDs compared with the other two GQDs. The above information establishes a foundation for understanding the relationship between material properties and toxicity in subsequent studies. Notably, the fluorescence properties of the three GQDs shown that N-GQDs had the largest Stokes shift, which implied better photostability and imaging brightness [21]. Therefore, without considering toxicity, N-GQDs have advantages over OH-GQDs and NH2-GQDs for biomedical imaging applications due to their low background interference, strong tissue penetration, and high sensitivity.

As the initial step in the interaction between nanomaterials and cells, and a prerequisite for cellular disturbances, the uptake of the three GQDs by liver macrophages was analyzed first. The finding that KCs took up the same amount of each GQD suggests similar uptake capacities of KCs for the three GQDs. The resulting damage caused by each GQD, with similar amounts inside KCs, was further examined in terms of cell viability, cell membrane damage, and cell morphology. The cell viability results indicated that, among OH-GQDs, N-GQDs, and NH2-GQDs, OH-GQDs had the most significant impact on KCs viability, while NH2-GQDs exhibited the least toxicity. Although there is currently a lack of comparative studies on the toxicity of these three types of GQDs, Yuan et al. found that even at concentrations as high as 200 μg/mL, the survival rate of A549 cells exposed to NH2-GQDs remained around 98 % [22]. The study of Tian also reported that OH-GQDs reduced cell viability in a concentration-dependent manner, with the survival rate of A549 cells dropping to 76.8 % at a concentration of 100 μg/mL [23]. Both of these studies, which are based on the same cell line, further demonstrate the lower cytotoxicity of NH2-GQDs compared to OH-GQDs. Moreover, the increased LDH release and observable cell rupture and swelling after OH-GQDs exposure indicate significant KCs damage caused by OH-GQDs. These findings suggest that the three types of GQDs have distinct toxicity to liver macrophage (OH-GQDs > N-GQDs > NH2-GQDs), and this difference is not due to the amount of uptake by KCs.

Transcriptomic analysis, which typically involves identifying differential genes and key signaling pathways to explore specific biological processes, is widely used to reveal the molecular mechanisms of NMs toxicity. To understand the rules of the effects caused by GQDs on KCs, we used transcriptomic sequencing to investigate the key biological processes and molecular mechanisms underlying GQD-induced KCs damage. In this study, three types of GQDs collectively influenced the extracellular matrix production, cell adhesion, proliferation, binding and signal transduction of macrophages, and these processes were coordinated to maintain the macrophage immune response. As exogenous substances, GQDs are recognized and phagocytosed by KCs, triggering an immune response. However, the degree and direction of immune response caused by different GQDs were not consistent according to the number of DEGs and the abnormal degree of inflammation-related genes. Among them, N-GQDs and NH2-GQDs caused changes in cell stress-related genes, but did not mediate serious inflammatory response. In contrast, OH-GQDs up-regulated a number of genes related to cellular inflammation (M1 polarization) and calcium homeostasis imbalance, and mediated the pro-inflammatory response of macrophages. Combined with the existing studies, it is known that quantum dots can regulate the direction of macrophage immune response or induce macrophage phenotype transformation by regulating the secretion of extracellular matrix components, affecting the binding of receptors on the surface of macrophages, changing cell migration and proliferation, which is one of the main macrophage perturbations caused by quantum dots. In addition to the pro-inflammatory responses of OH-GQDs found in this study, a GQD-based drug delivery system was shown to promote the transformation of M1 to M2 macrophages and be used for the treatment of inflammatory arthritis [24]. To better promote the safe application and clinical translation of GQDs, it is essential to clarify the pro-inflammatory mechanism and toxicity sources of OH-GQDs. Further KEGG pathway enrichment analysis identified the NOD-like receptor signaling pathway as the most significantly affected pathway in KCs after OH-GQDs exposure. Additionally, through DEG interaction networks, we identified several calcium homeostasis-related genes (Cask, Calhm6, IRF1, and Casr) that play central roles in OH-GQDs-induced liver macrophage damage. For instance, CALHM6, as a member of the calcium homeostasis regulation family, may link cellular calcium imbalance to macrophage pro-inflammatory responses. In fact, inflammation and calcium homeostasis imbalance induced by nanomaterials are not uncommon. For example, CdSe/ZnS QDs and SiO2 nanoparticles trigger inflammatory responses in L02 cells, accompanied by an increase in intracellular Ca2+ and activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome [25,26]. GO and CNTs can also trigger phospholipase C activation through lipid peroxidation, leading to increased calcium flux, mitochondrial ROS production, and inflammatory responses [27,28]. The transcriptomic results and existing studies suggest that calcium homeostasis imbalance and NOD-like receptor-mediated inflammatory responses may be key events in OH-GQDs-induced liver macrophage damage, warranting further investigation.

NLRP3 is a key regulatory protein in the NOD-like receptor signaling, capable of recognizing specific pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) and damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) [29]. The NLRP3 inflammasome, composed of NLRP3, ASC, and Pro-caspase-1, acts as a trigger for inflammatory responses and plays a crucial role in liver injury, including the progression of liver diseases such as fibrosis, cirrhosis, and liver cancer[30]. Both metal-containing and metal-free QDs have been shown to activate the NLRP3 inflammasome, mediating cellular inflammatory responses, like CdSe/ZnS QDs inducing NLRP3 inflammasome-dependent inflammation in L02 cells and MoS2 QDs activating NLRP3 inflammasome by triggering NF-κB and mtROS in BV2 cells. In this study, we not only confirmed the significant activation of the NOD-like receptor pathway at the transcriptional level, but also validated it at the translational level by examining changes in the key proteins, including NLRP3, Pro-caspase-1, Caspase-1, and IL-1β, which included increased protein expression following GQDs exposure, as well as reduced IL-1β levels after pretreatment with an NLRP3 inhibitor. The subsequent generation of IL-1β and IL-18 further initiates an inflammatory signaling cascade. As we all know, IL-1β, a critical pro-inflammatory cytokine, can bind to its receptor to induce the expression of various inflammatory cytokines, such as TNF-α and IL-6, thereby amplifying the inflammatory response. Our study also confirmed that exposure to 25 μg/mL GQDs induced inflammation in KCs, as evidenced by elevated levels of cytokines (including IL-1β, IL-18, TNF-α, and IL-6) in the cell culture supernatant. These findings suggest that an excessive inflammatory response is one of the primary toxic outcomes of GQDs, especially OH-GQDs, in mediating liver macrophage damage, with NLRP3 inflammasome activation being a critical molecular mechanism.

Calcium plays a role in virtually all life processes, and Ca2+, often called the “second messenger” in cells, controls various cellular processes, including proliferation and differentiation, transcription, cell metabolism, and cell death [31]. Our previous transcriptomic analysis indicated that GQDs can cause intracellular Ca2+ imbalance. Therefore, we further examined the temporal changes of Ca2+ levels within KCs after exposure to different GQDs, finding a significant increase in intracellular Ca2+ levels 3 h post-exposure, with the most pronounced changes caused by OH-GQDs. These findings are consistent with previous studies. Tang et al. discovered that unmodified CdSe QDs increase cytoplasmic calcium levels in primary hippocampal neurons through extracellular Ca2+ influx and intracellular Ca2+ release [32]. Another study on the hepatotoxicity of CdTe QDs also indicated that CdTe QDs elevated hepatic Ca2+ content in hepatocyte [33]. Notably, in this study, we observed that the GQD-induced Ca2+ elevation in KCs peaks at 6–12 hours post-exposure and then declines. This may be attributed to the excessive cytoplasmic Ca2+ being taken up by mitochondria via the mitochondrial calcium uniporter, resulting in mitochondrial dysfunction, a point that will be explored further in subsequent investigations. Although many studies have clarified the relationship between calcium homeostasis imbalance and NLRP3 inflammasome activation as well as inflammation, for instance, Tomohiko et al. suggested that various substances (e.g., Nigericin, MSU, and alum) induced Ca2+ signaling to activate the NLRP3 inflammasome [34]. The mechanistic link between the two in GQD-induced liver macrophage damage still requires confirmation. Therefore, we conducted a reverse confirmation using the intracellular Ca2+ chelator BAPTA-AM, observing that chelating excess cytoplasmic Ca2+ significantly suppressed KCs inflammation, suggesting that cytoplasmic Ca2+ overload contributes to NLRP3 inflammasome-dependent inflammation induced by different GQDs.

While Ca2+ signaling is necessary for NLRP3 inflammasome activation, calcium homeostasis imbalance is not always the direct trigger for NLRP3 activation; rather, the connection often occurs through subcellular organelle disturbances, particularly mitochondrial dysfunction [34]. Mitochondria, as critical cellular organelles, play a role in energy metabolism, with mitochondrial calcium dynamics being vital for cellular energetics and survival. Mitochondrial Ca2+ regulates dehydrogenases in the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle, thereby controlling ATP synthesis. However, excessively high Ca2+ concentrations in the mitochondrial matrix can increase mtROS levels, leading to mitochondrial membrane depolarization. In this study, after treating KCs with OH-GQDs, N-GQDs, and NH2-GQDs, we observed varying degrees of mitochondrial damage across the three GQD types, which manifested as mitochondrial gene abnormalities, increased mitochondrial Ca2+ level, MMP depolarization, elevated mtROS, and reduced ATP content, with OH-GQDs causing more severe mitochondrial damage than N-GQDs and NH2-GQDs. These findings indicate that mitochondria are among the subcellular organelles most significantly affected in GQDs-induced hepatocyte toxicity.

In fact, many studies have reported the mitochondrial damage caused by QDs, but the more significant finding in this work is the role of GQD-induced mitochondrial dysfunction as an intermediary between cellular calcium imbalance and inflammation. Existing research indicates that physiological Ca2+ uptake enhances mitochondrial metabolism, but excessive and/or sustained Ca2+ influx can trigger mitochondrial Ca2+ overload, leading to mitochondrial dysfunction [35]. Taking OH-GQDs as an example, our study found that the increased Ca2+ within KCs gradually decreased after 12 hours of OH-GQD exposure, while mitochondrial Ca2+ in KCs significantly increased 24 hours post-exposure. Furthermore, when the excess cytoplasmic Ca2+ was chelated by BAPTA-AM, the GQD-mediated increase in mitochondrial Ca2+ content was inhibited, and the resulting abnormalities in MMP, mtROS, and ATP levels were also alleviated. These results suggest that in the process of GQDs-induced damage to liver macrophages, the excess cytoplasmic Ca2+ is absorbed by mitochondria, leading to mitochondrial Ca2+ overload and subsequent mitochondrial dysfunction.

Another question that needs to be addressed is how mitochondrial dysfunction induced by GQDs mediates NLRP3 inflammasome-dependent inflammation. Following mitochondrial damage, excessive mtROS has been confirmed as a direct factor in activating the NLRP3 inflammasome, with abundant evidence in studies on QDs-induced toxicity [36]. For instance, a study on the toxic mechanisms of Ag2Se QDs indicated that Ag2Se QDs trigger NLRP3 inflammasome activation through mtROS, causing active Caspase-1 to cleave the IL-1β precursor into mature IL-1β in BV2 cells [37]. Therefore, we pre-treated KCs with the mtROS scavenger Mito-Tempo for 2 h and observed that Mito-Tempo maintained normal expression levels of NLRP3 and inflammasome-related proteins in KCs, which suggested that mitochondrial Ca2+ overload-induced mtROS production activates the NLRP3 inflammasome, promoting inflammation.

Based on the above results, it can be known that calcium homeostasis imbalance plays a dominant role in the inflammatory damage of liver macrophages mediated by GQDs. However, the source of the large increase of Ca2+ in the cytoplasm remains unclear. Generally, intracellular Ca2+ is stored in the endoplasmic reticulum (ER), which maintains calcium homeostasis by controlling the levels of free and bound Ca2+ within cells. However, under various pathological factors, ER stress may lead to disrupted calcium signaling and adverse outcomes. For example, LPS stimulation of BV2 cells can deplete Ca2+ in ER, subsequently causing mitochondrial Ca2+ overload, ultimately leading to mitochondrial dysfunction and inflammation [38]. Similarly, E. coli infection led to an abnormal increase in cytoplasmic Ca2+ and mitochondrial dysfunction, where the increase in cytoplasmic Ca2+ mainly results from the release of Ca2+ stored in the ER [39]. Besides ER Ca2+ release into the cytoplasm, extracellular Ca2+ influx is also a source. Studies have shown that in pulmonary edema pathogenesis, Ca2+ influx from the extracellular medium through the TRPV1 channel elevates cytoplasmic Ca2+ concentration, which increases TNF-α and IL-1β release by enhancing NF-κB phosphorylation [40]. To clarify the source of Ca2+ in this study, we pre-treated cells with the ER Ca2+ channel inhibitor 2-APB and the extracellular Ca2+ chelator EGTA, subsequently measuring the Ca2+ levels in KCs and the subsequent NLRP3 inflammatory response. Interestingly, we found that 2-APB could only partially restored the increase in cytoplasmic Ca2+ caused by OH-GQDs in KCs, while EGTA alleviated calcium homeostasis imbalance induced by all three GQDs. These results suggested that the Ca2+ influx triggered by N-GQDs and NH2-GQDs primarily came from extracellular medium, while the increase in cytoplasmic Ca2+ induced by OH-GQDs in KCs was mainly from the calcium release from ER in addition to the extracellular pathway.

Our research further focused on the reasons behind ER calcium release triggered by OH-GQDs. In nanomaterials, the oxygen functional group content affects oxidative capacity in vitro, potentially due to the introduction of surface defects that provide additional reactive sites, thereby enhancing the surface reactivity and non-biological radical generation of GQDs [41]. Notably, the hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxyl (-COOH) groups on OH-GQDs act as redox-active catalytic centers, mimicking the activity of glutathione peroxidase (GPx). These functional groups facilitate electron transfer reactions, and interact with GSH through thiol-disulfide exchange or hydrogen bonding, converting reduced GSH to oxidized glutathione (GSSG) while regenerating oxygen radicals (•OH and O2•−) in a self-sustaining cycle. This mechanism aligns with previous reports showing that oxygen-rich carbon nanomaterials exhibit intrinsic peroxidase-like activity due to their ability to catalyze oxygen radicals via surface-mediated redox cycling [42,43]. Our findings support this by showing that OH-GQDs, which contain the highest oxygen functional group content, exhibit significant oxygen radical generation capabilities. This enzyme-like behavior is further evidenced by their ability to deplete glutathione via catalytic oxidation. In contrast, N-GQDs and NH₂-GQDs lack significant oxygen-containing moieties. Nitrogen doping in N-GQDs introduces electron-rich pyrrolic/pyridinic N configurations, which stabilize charge distribution and reduce redox reactivity. Similarly, NH₂-GQDs feature amine groups (-NH₂) that exhibit lower electron affinity compared to -OH/-COOH, thereby diminishing their capacity to initiate radical chain reactions.

GSH, the most abundant thiol-containing small molecule within cells, is crucial for scavenging oxygen radicals and maintaining intracellular redox balance. In the ER, a high GSH/GSSG ratio preserves an oxidizing environment necessary for disulfide bond formation and proper protein folding. Once GSH depletion triggers ER stress, the ER, one of the primary intracellular calcium reservoirs, can release Ca2+ into the cytoplasm via IP3R (inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate receptor) or RYR (ryanodine receptor), increasing cytosolic calcium levels [44]. The catalytic depletion of GSH by OH-GQDs exacerbates cell stress, creating a feedforward loop that amplifies mitochondrial damage and ER calcium release. In this study, we observed that 25 μg/mL OH-GQDs induced GSH depletion in both non-biological and biological systems, upregulating the ER stress markers CHOP and GRP78, thus identifying GSH depletion as the molecular initiating event leading to the excessive inflammatory response in KCs triggered by OH-GQDs.

Many existing studies report the adverse effects of nanomaterials on GSH, the ER, and mitochondria, yet most focus solely on these adverse outcomes without linking the upstream and downstream mechanisms. In contrast, from the perspective of intracellular Ca2+, we clarified how OH-GQD-mediated GSH depletion triggered ER stress and induced calcium release from the ER into the cytosol, which was subsequently absorbed by mitochondria, resulting in mitochondrial dysfunction and NLRP3-dependent inflammation. Taking calcium ions as a clue, this study investigated the cascade of subcellular organelle damage within cells, detailing the toxicological reaction chain through which GQDs, particularly OH-GQDs, induce inflammatory responses in KCs. This approach provides a comprehensive perspective on the molecular mechanisms of toxicity, highlighting that nanomaterials may initiate cellular chain reactions rather than isolated toxic endpoints once internalized. Another significant advancement of this study is the identification of oxygen functional groups and their associated high oxygen radical generation capacity as primary factors driving GQD-induced inflammation. By selectively modifying or functionalizing OH-GQDs to regulate their oxygen radical generation capacity without compromising their photoluminescent properties, it may be possible to enhance the biocompatibility of OH-GQDs. This strategy holds promise for developing safer and more effective GQDs for biomedical applications.

While our study primarily utilized the KUP5 mouse KC cell line—a well-established model for studying nanoparticle-induced immune responses due to its retained phagocytic activity and cytokine secretion profiles, we acknowledge the importance of corroborating our findings in primary or human-derived KCs to enhance translational relevance [45,46]. Consistent with our results, prior studies assessing QDs toxicity in human or primary KC models reveal the core toxicity mechanism of quantum dots is involved calcium dysregulation, mitochondrial dysfunction and inflammation [47–49]. This suggesting that macrophage responses to QDs are conserved across species and cell models, reinforcing the validity of our findings in KUP5 cells. Nevertheless, we recognize the limitations of cell lines. Future work will prioritize human KC models, such as primary liver-resident macrophages from perfused donor tissues, to further validate these mechanisms. Moreover, our current data focus on KC-intrinsic mechanisms, but GQD-induced KC activation may trigger cross-talk with other hepatic cell types. For example, inflammatory signals from KCs (e.g., IL-1β) can impair hepatocyte function, leading to metabolic dysregulation or apoptosis. Future work should employ co-culture models (e.g., KC-HSC-LSEC tri-cultures) or in vivo models to dissect how GQD-triggered KC inflammation propagates across the liver ecosystem.

In this study, we demonstrated that the toxicity of the three types of GQDs toward KCs followed the order OH-GQDs > N-GQDs > NH2-GQDs, with inflammation being the primary form of toxic damage. The inflammatory damage in KCs induced by 25 μg/mL GQDs largely resulted from GQDs-induced cytoplasmic calcium imbalance, leading to mitochondrial Ca2+ overload, mitochondrial dysfunction, and mtROS generation, which further triggers NLRP3 inflammasome-dependent inflammation. Unlike the other two GQDs, OH-GQDs-induced calcium imbalance came not only from extracellular Ca2+ influx but also relied on ER stress-mediated ER Ca2+ release. The depletion of GSH by oxygen free radicals generated by OH-GQDs is the molecular initiating event of a series of toxic cascade events. This underscores that the fundamental cause of toxicity differences among the GQDs lies in their varying oxygen functional group content and associated oxygen radical-generating capacities. From the perspective of nanomaterial safe-by-design, our findings suggest that targeting oxygen functional group content and radical generation capacity may an important strategy to improve the biocompatibility of GQDs, especially OH-GQDs, which is of great significance for the development of non-toxic, and high-performance nano-diagnostic and therapeutic agents.
